examples.title: ID3 Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for ID3 decision tree induction algorithm using Information Gain
examples.code:
FUNCTION ID3(examples, target_attribute, attributes):
 // Base cases
 IF examples is empty: RETURN majority_class(parent_examples)
 IF all examples have same class: RETURN that class
 IF attributes is empty: RETURN majority_class(examples)
 
 // Select best attribute
 A ← attribute with highest Information_Gain(examples, attributes)
 tree ← new decision node with A as root
 
 // Create branches for each value
 FOR EACH value vₖ IN values_of(A):
 examplesₖ ← {e ∈ examples where e.A = vₖ}
 IF examplesₖ is empty:
 tree.child[vₖ] ← majority_class(examples)
 ELSE:
 tree.child[vₖ] ← ID3(examplesₖ, target_attribute, attributes - {A})
 
 RETURN tree

FUNCTION Information_Gain(examples, attribute):
 H_before ← Entropy(examples, target_attribute)
 weighted_H_after ← 0
 FOR EACH value v IN values_of(attribute):
 examples_v ← {e ∈ examples where e.attribute = v}
 weight ← |examples_v| / |examples|
 weighted_H_after ← weighted_H_after + weight × Entropy(examples_v, target_attribute)
 RETURN H_before - weighted_H_after
examples.explanation: ID3 recursively builds a decision tree by selecting the attribute with highest Information Gain at each step. The algorithm stops when all examples belong to one class or no attributes remain.

examples.title: CART Binary Splitting for Continuous Attributes
examples.description: Algorithm for finding optimal binary split point in CART for continuous attributes
examples.code:
FUNCTION Find_Optimal_Split_CART(examples, continuous_attribute, target):
 // Sort examples by the continuous attribute
 sorted_examples ← Sort(examples, continuous_attribute)
 n ← |sorted_examples|
 
 best_gain ← 0
 best_threshold ← None
 
 // Evaluate all possible thresholds (midpoints between consecutive values)
 FOR i FROM 1 TO n-1:
 // Only consider split points where class changes (optional optimization)
 IF sorted_examples[i].target ≠ sorted_examples[i+1].target:
 threshold ← (sorted_examples[i].continuous_attr + 
 sorted_examples[i+1].continuous_attr) / 2
 
 // Create left and right partitions
 left ← sorted_examples[1..i]
 right ← sorted_examples[i+1..n]
 
 // Calculate Gini reduction
 gini_parent ← Gini_Impurity(examples, target)
 gini_left ← Gini_Impurity(left, target)
 gini_right ← Gini_Impurity(right, target)
 
 weighted_gini ← (|left|/n) × gini_left + (|right|/n) × gini_right
 gain ← gini_parent - weighted_gini
 
 IF gain > best_gain:
 best_gain ← gain
 best_threshold ← threshold
 
 RETURN (best_threshold, best_gain)
examples.explanation: CART finds optimal binary splits for continuous attributes by sorting values and evaluating the Gini impurity reduction at each possible midpoint between consecutive sorted values. This ensures finding the globally optimal split for that attribute.

examples.title: Cost-Complexity Pruning (CCP)
examples.description: Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning algorithm used in CART for post-pruning
examples.explanation: Cost-Complexity Pruning (CCP) generates a sequence of increasingly pruned trees, each optimal for some complexity parameter α. The best tree is selected based on validation error or cross-validation, balancing model complexity against training error.
